heading: Challenges
label: 1870 Challenges
text: In civil cases, each party shall be entitled to three peremptory challenges. Several defendants or several plaintiffs may be considered as a single party for the purposes of making challenges, or the court may allow additional peremptory challenges and permit them to be exercised separately or jointly.All challenges for cause or favor, whether to the array or panel or to individual jurors, shall be determined by the court.
notes: Historical and Revision NotesBased on title 28, U.S.C., 1940 ed., § 424 (, ).Mar. 3, 1911, ch. 231, § 28736 Stat. 1166Provisions of , U.S.C., 1940 ed., relating to the number of peremptory challenges in criminal cases were deleted as superseded by Rule 24 of the Federal Rules of Criminal Procedure.section 424 of title 28The last sentence of the first paragraph was added to permit the same flexibility in the matter of challenges in civil cases as is permitted in criminal cases by said Rule 24.Words “without aid of triers” at end of , U.S.C., 1940 ed., were omitted as surplusage.section 424 of title 28Changes were made in phraseology.
Amendments1959— substituted “may” for “shall” after “several plaintiffs”, and “, or the court may allow” for “. If there is more than one defendant the court may allow the defendants”.Pub. L. 86–282
